hypothesis: Bollinger bands identify extreme price levels while StochRSI confirms overbought/oversold conditions; combining them forces entries only when price is at a band edge and the oscillator signals a reversal, reducing noise in a mean‑reverting market. ATR filtering ensures trades are taken only in sufficient volatility, limiting false signals during low‑activity periods.

Williams %R and StochRSI extremes flag temporary overbought or oversold conditions; mean reversion brings price back to the neutral zone, so taking positions at these extremes while using ATR to set dynamic stops and profits adapts risk to market volatility and filters out weak moves

When Williams %R reaches extreme values, price tends to revert to its recent high/low range; combining this with a StochRSI confirmation of overbought/oversold momentum reduces false entries, while an ATR filter ensures trades only occur in sufficiently volatile conditions, improving risk-adjusted performance.

When MATICUSDC price reaches the extreme ends of the Williams %R, the market tends to revert toward the middle of the %R range. Combining this with a confirming StochRSI overbought/oversold signal reduces false reversals, while ATR-based risk limits protect against sudden volatility spikes.

Mean‑reversion works best when the price is far from its typical range and the market is not in a strong trend. By entering only when Williams %R hits extreme oversold/overbought levels AND StochRSI confirms the same extreme, we capture likely pullbacks. Exiting when the %R moves toward its neutral zone or StochRSI crosses 50 ensures we close before the reversal unwinds, while ATR‑based SL/TP protects against unexpected volatility spikes.
